@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Sports and Team Games
@graph.description: The ball bounces off. The swing misses. The kick goes sideways. He's watching from the sideline while other kids play. Sports skills can be taught — even when coordination is hard. Here are 9 materials that help.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/gross motor skills / physical education / social-motor integration / team participation / adaptive sports / peer interaction/sports skills development / team game participation / motor coordination for athletics / social aspects of sports / inclusive physical activity/9-materials-that-help-with-sports-and-team-games#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does sports have become my son's biggest source of social pain. he desperately wants to play with other kids, but his body doesn't cooperate. catching is terrifying for him — he flinches, closes his eyes, and the ball bounces away. throwing is wildly inaccurate. kicking goes the wrong direction. batting? he swings when the ball is still meters away or after it's already passed. it's like there's a disconnect between what he sees and what his body does. the timing is always off. the team game rules confuse him too. he doesn't understand when to run, when to pass, whose turn it is, what happens after someone scores. while other kids seem to just 'get it,' he stands there looking lost. when he does get the ball, he panics and either freezes or does the wrong thing. the social part is almost worse than the physical part. he can't read when teammates are open for a pass. he doesn't know how to call for the ball. when he makes mistakes, he doesn't know how to handle it — sometimes he melts down, sometimes he just walks off. the other kids have noticed he's different. they've stopped inviting him to play. they groan when he's put on their team. one boy told him he 'ruins the game.' i've tried everything i can think of. we practice at home, but i'm not a sports coach. i don't know how to teach him to catch when standard methods don't work. the local sports clubs won't take him because he 'disrupts the group.' he needs to build these skills somehow, but the opportunities to practice with peers are disappearing. pe class at school is becoming a weekly source of dread?

acceptedAnswer.text: MOTOR COORDINATION DISORDER / DEVELOPMENTAL COORDINATION DISORDER (DCD) / DYSPRAXIA / GROSS MOTOR SKILL DELAY / VISUAL-MOTOR INTEGRATION DEFICITS / SPORTS SKILL ACQUISITION DIFFICULTIES! Difficulty with sports and team games often reflects underlying challenges in motor coordination, visual-motor integration, motor planning, timing, spatial awareness, and the integration of multiple skills simultaneously. Children may struggle with ball skills (catching, throwing, kicking, hitting), locomotor coordination (running, jumping, agility), body awareness in space, predicting object trajectories, and timing their movements to match moving objects. Additionally, team games require understanding complex rules, reading social cues from teammates, managing emotions during competition, and integrating motor skills while simultaneously processing social and cognitive demands. For children with developmental coordination disorder (DCD), autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, or other developmental differences, these challenges can lead to exclusion from peer activities, reduced physical activity, lower self-esteem, and loss of important social opportunities that occur during play and sports.

mainEntity.name: What is Motor Coordination Disorder / Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) / Dyspraxia / Gross Motor Skill Delay / Visual-Motor Integration Deficits / Sports Skill Acquisition Difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Difficulty with sports and team games often reflects underlying challenges in motor coordination, visual-motor integration, motor planning, timing, spatial awareness, and the integration of multiple skills simultaneously. Children may struggle with ball skills (catching, throwing, kicking, hitting), locomotor coordination (running, jumping, agility), body awareness in space, predicting object trajectories, and timing their movements to match moving objects. Additionally, team games require understanding complex rules, reading social cues from teammates, managing emotions during competition, and integrating motor skills while simultaneously processing social and cognitive demands. For children with developmental coordination disorder (DCD), autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, or other developmental differences, these challenges can lead to exclusion from peer activities, reduced physical activity, lower self-esteem, and loss of important social opportunities that occur during play and sports. Common signs include: Difficulty catching — flinching, closing eyes, poor hand positioning, Throwing with inaccurate aim or poor force modulation, Kicking that misses or goes wrong direction, Batting/hitting timing consistently off, Running that appears awkward or uncoordinated.

mainEntity.name: How does a Modified Sports Balls (Size, Weight, Speed Variations) help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard sports balls are designed for typical motor development — they move at speeds and require reactions that struggling children can't yet manage. Modified balls change the equation. Larger balls are easier to track and catch. Lighter balls move slower, giving more reaction time. Textured balls provide better grip feedback. Foam balls reduce fear of impact pain. Balloon balls move very slowly, allowing children to practice the motion pattern before adding speed. By starting with balls matched to the child's current ability, you build success and confidence while gradually progressing to standard equipment. This is skill scaffolding — meeting the child where they are rather than where they 'should' be. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Ball modification strategies: START WITH: Large beach balls (slow, easy to see, no pain fear), balloon balls, foam balls, large soft fabric balls. PROGRESS THROUGH: Gradually smaller sizes, gradually more weight, gradually faster speeds. HOMEMADE OPTIONS: Stuff a sock and tie it (soft ball), plastic bag ball (slow-moving), balloon inside fabric bag (larger + soft), newspaper ball (light, no pain). KEY PRINCIPLE: Find the size/weight/speed where child succeeds 80%+ of the time. That's their current level. Only increase difficulty when success is consistent. A child who fears the ball will never learn to catch it — fear must be removed first with appropriate equipment. Safety: Ensure modified balls are age-appropriate and don't pose choking hazards for younger children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Tracking and Prediction Training Tools help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Catching and hitting require predicting where a moving object will be — not where it is now, but where it will arrive. Many children with sports difficulties have challenges with visual tracking (following moving objects smoothly) and motion prediction. Training tools develop these prerequisite skills before adding the motor demands of actually catching. Tracking activities might include following a moving light, tracking a ball on a string, or watching objects roll. Prediction activities involve guessing where something will end up before it arrives. These visual-motor foundations must be solid before sports skills can develop. Without good tracking and prediction, a child is essentially catching blind. Price: ₹150–600. DIY option: Visual tracking training: TRACKING PRACTICE: Ball on string — swing slowly, child follows with eyes only (head still). Flashlight on wall — move slowly, child tracks. Roll ball across floor — child watches until it stops. Bubble tracking — follow single bubbles. PREDICTION PRACTICE: Roll ball toward targets — 'Which target will it hit?' before it arrives. Toss ball up — 'Point to where it will land' before it lands. Ramp rolling — predict which track ball will exit. Ball through tube — 'Where will it come out?' Balloon toss — slow motion allows prediction practice. PROGRESSION: Start slow, start predictable, gradually add speed and variability. Practice eyes-only before adding any motor response. Safety: Watch for visual fatigue. Short sessions with breaks. Some children may need vision assessment first.

mainEntity.name: How does a Catching Development Progression Equipment help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Catching isn't one skill — it's a developmental progression. Children first learn to trap a rolled ball against their body. Then catch with two hands against chest. Then catch with hands only, away from body. Then catch with one hand. Then catch while moving. Equipment supports each stage of this progression. Scoops and mitts extend the catching surface, making success easier. Velcro catch sets eliminate the need for perfect hand closure. Rebound nets allow self-practice. Catching tubes guide the ball into hands. Each piece of equipment is a stepping stone, not a permanent crutch — used until the child develops enough skill to progress to the next level. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Catching progression: STAGE 1 — TRAPPING: Roll ball, child traps against body with arms. Sit with legs in V, roll ball into V. No hands catching needed — just stopping. STAGE 2 — TWO HANDS + BODY: Toss gently, catch against chest. Hands plus body as backboard. STAGE 3 — HANDS ONLY: Scoop/mitt first (extends catching surface), then velcro (eliminates timing of hand close), then bare hands with balloon/scarves, then soft balls, then standard. STAGE 4 — ONE HAND: Only after two-hand mastery. Start with larger items. STAGE 5 — WHILE MOVING: Stationary first, walking, then running. EQUIPMENT: Large scoops, catching mitts, velcro paddle sets, rebound net for self-practice, catching tubes. KEY: Don't skip stages. Master each before progressing. Safety: Build confidence slowly. Fear of the ball prevents learning — use soft equipment until fear resolves.

mainEntity.name: How does a Batting and Hitting Equipment (Modified Bats, Tees, Targets) help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Hitting a moving ball requires visual tracking, prediction, timing, and motor execution — all in a split second. This is extremely challenging for children with coordination difficulties. Modified equipment removes variables to isolate and train each component. Batting tees remove the timing element entirely — child can focus on swing mechanics without tracking. Larger bats extend the hitting surface. Lighter bats allow faster, more controlled swings. Suspended balls allow practice with minimal speed. Slow-pitch or underhand serves reduce tracking demands. By systematically building each component, children develop the timing and coordination that eventually allows hitting standard pitches. Price: ₹400–1,500. DIY option: Hitting skill development: STAGE 1 — STATIONARY: Batting tee — child hits ball off tee. Focus purely on swing mechanics. No timing, no tracking. Hit until consistent solid contact. STAGE 2 — SUSPENDED: Ball on string, hanging still. Swing through. Then gently swing the ball and hit. STAGE 3 — ROLLED: Ball rolled on ground, child hits with bat. Simpler tracking than airborne. STAGE 4 — SLOW AIR: Underhand lob from short distance, very slow arc, very predictable. STAGE 5 — STANDARD: Gradually increase speed, distance, variability. BAT MODIFICATIONS: Start wider and shorter (easier control). Foam bats, plastic bats before wood/metal. KEY: Hundreds of tee hits before pitched balls. Muscle memory develops from repetition. Safety: Ensure adequate space for batting practice. Use appropriate safety equipment.

mainEntity.name: How does a Kicking Targets and Guides help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Kicking combines balance (standing on one leg), power generation, aim, and foot-ball contact — a complex motor sequence. Children with coordination difficulties often struggle with accuracy, force modulation, or the balance required. Kicking targets provide visual goals that motivate and give feedback. Ground guides show where to place the ball and where to aim. Tethered balls prevent constant retrieval and allow focus on repetition. Pop-up goals provide satisfying feedback when hit. By making the target clear and providing immediate feedback, children can self-adjust and improve through practice. Without targets, kicking is aimless; with them, each kick has purpose and feedback. Price: ₹300–1,000. DIY option: Kicking skill development: TARGETS: Empty plastic bottles to knock down, pop-up cones, targets with Velcro balls that stick, numbered zones for points, goals with noise makers. GROUND GUIDES: Tape/chalk circle for ball placement, footprint for standing foot, arrow for aim direction, 'kick zone' marked on ball. PROGRESSION: Large target close → smaller target close → large target far → smaller target far → moving targets → game situations. TETHERED KICKING: Ball in net bag on rope — kick, ball returns. Or ball tethered to stake. Allows high repetition without retrieval. FORCE PRACTICE: 'Gentle' zone close, 'hard' zone far — learn to modulate. Different colored targets for force levels. Safety: Ensure targets are stable and won't cause injury when struck. Clear space around kicking area.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Rule Cards and Game Sequence Strips help with motor coordination disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Team games have complex rules that typical children absorb through observation and experience. Children with developmental differences often need rules made explicit and visual. Visual rule cards show what to do in specific situations — 'When you catch the ball: throw to first base.' Game sequence strips show the order of play — 'Batter hits → run to first → if caught, out.' Without this explicit instruction, children may understand individual skills but remain lost during actual games. Visual supports also help in the moment — when confused, child can check the card rather than freezing or doing the wrong thing. This reduces anxiety and increases participation confidence. Price: ₹100–400. DIY option: Creating game visual supports: RULE CARDS: For each game, identify key decision points. Create cards: 'IF [situation] THEN [action].' Example: Cricket fielding — 'If you catch the ball in the air, the batter is out.' 'If the ball goes past you, get it and throw to the keeper.' SEQUENCE STRIPS: Show order of game flow. Who does what, in what order. Include what child specifically does at each stage. POSITION GUIDES: Visual diagram of field with child's position marked. Arrows showing where to throw, where to run. WEARABLE: Laminate key rules on wristband or lanyard for in-game reference. BEFORE GAME: Review cards at home. Walk through on empty field. Practice specific situations before they occur in games. Safety: Keep cards simple and limited to most important rules. Too much information overwhelms.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Motor Coordination Disorder
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Sports skill difficulties often reflect underlying motor coordination challenges, visual-motor integration deficits, or motor planning difficulties — not laziness or lack of interest. Children with Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD), autism, ADHD, and other developmental differences frequently struggle with the complex motor demands of sports. These skills can be improved with systematic, scaffolded instruction that breaks down complex skills into learnable components, uses modified equipment to enable success, and progressively builds toward standard participation. The goal is functional participation and access to peer physical play — not athletic excellence. Early intervention prevents the negative spiral of avoidance, reduced practice, increased gap, and further exclusion.

supply.name: Modified Sports Balls (Size, Weight, Speed Variations)
Visual Tracking and Prediction Training Tools

Additionally, team games require understanding complex rules, reading social cues from teammates, managing emotions during competition, and integrating motor skills while simultaneously processing social and cognitive demands. For children with developmental coordination disorder (DCD), autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, or other developmental differences, these challenges can lead to exclusion from peer activities, reduced physical activity, lower self-esteem, and loss of important social opportunities that occur during play and sports. item.description: Standard sports balls are designed for typical motor development — they move at speeds and require reactions that struggling children can't yet manage. By starting with balls matched to the child's current ability, you build success and confidence while gradually progressing to standard equipment. Without good tracking and prediction, a child is essentially catching blind.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Visual tracking training: TRACKING PRACTICE: Ball on string — swing slowly, child follows with eyes only (head still). Flashlight on wall — move slowly, child tracks. Roll ball across floor — child watches until it stops. Bubble tracking — follow single bubbles. PREDICTION PRACTICE: Roll ball toward targets — 'Which target will it hit?' before it arrives. Toss ball up — 'Point to where it will land' before it lands. Ramp rolling — predict which track ball will exit. Ball through tube — 'Where will it come out?' Balloon toss — slow motion allows prediction practice. PROGRESSION: Start slow, start predictable, gradually add speed and variability. Practice eyes-only before adding any motor response.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Watch for visual fatigue. Short sessions with breaks. Some children may need vision assessment first.

item.name: Catching Development Progression Equipment
item.description: Catching isn't one skill — it's a developmental progression. Children first learn to trap a rolled ball against their body. Then catch with two hands against chest. Then catch with hands only, away from body. Then catch with one hand. Then catch while moving. Equipment supports each stage of this progression. Scoops and mitts extend the catching surface, making success easier. Velcro catch sets eliminate the need for perfect hand closure. Rebound nets allow self-practice. Catching tubes guide the ball into hands. Each piece of equipment is a stepping stone, not a permanent crutch — used until the child develops enough skill to progress to the next level.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Catching progression: STAGE 1 — TRAPPING: Roll ball, child traps against body with arms. Sit with legs in V, roll ball into V. No hands catching needed — just stopping. STAGE 2 — TWO HANDS + BODY: Toss gently, catch against chest. Hands plus body as backboard. STAGE 3 — HANDS ONLY: Scoop/mitt first (extends catching surface), then velcro (eliminates timing of hand close), then bare hands with balloon/scarves, then soft balls, then standard. STAGE 4 — ONE HAND: Only after two-hand mastery. Start with larger items. STAGE 5 — WHILE MOVING: Stationary first, walking, then running. EQUIPMENT: Large scoops, catching mitts, velcro paddle sets, rebound net for self-practice, catching tubes. KEY: Don't skip stages. Master each before progressing.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Build confidence slowly. Fear of the ball prevents learning — use soft equipment until fear resolves.

item.name: Batting and Hitting Equipment (Modified Bats, Tees, Targets)
item.description: Hitting a moving ball requires visual tracking, prediction, timing, and motor execution — all in a split second. This is extremely challenging for children with coordination difficulties. Modified equipment removes variables to isolate and train each component. Batting tees remove the timing element entirely — child can focus on swing mechanics without tracking. Larger bats extend the hitting surface. Lighter bats allow faster, more controlled swings. Suspended balls allow practice with minimal speed. Slow-pitch or underhand serves reduce tracking demands. By systematically building each component, children develop the timing and coordination that eventually allows hitting standard pitches.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Hitting skill development: STAGE 1 — STATIONARY: Batting tee — child hits ball off tee. Focus purely on swing mechanics. No timing, no tracking. Hit until consistent solid contact. STAGE 2 — SUSPENDED: Ball on string, hanging still. Swing through. Then gently swing the ball and hit. STAGE 3 — ROLLED: Ball rolled on ground, child hits with bat. Simpler tracking than airborne. STAGE 4 — SLOW AIR: Underhand lob from short distance, very slow arc, very predictable. STAGE 5 — STANDARD: Gradually increase speed, distance, variability. BAT MODIFICATIONS: Start wider and shorter (easier control). Foam bats, plastic bats before wood/metal. KEY: Hundreds of tee hits before pitched balls. Muscle memory develops from repetition.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate space for batting practice. Use appropriate safety equipment.

item.name: Kicking Targets and Guides
item.description: Kicking combines balance (standing on one leg), power generation, aim, and foot-ball contact — a complex motor sequence. Children with coordination difficulties often struggle with accuracy, force modulation, or the balance required. Kicking targets provide visual goals that motivate and give feedback. Ground guides show where to place the ball and where to aim. Tethered balls prevent constant retrieval and allow focus on repetition. Pop-up goals provide satisfying feedback when hit. By making the target clear and providing immediate feedback, children can self-adjust and improve through practice. Without targets, kicking is aimless; with them, each kick has purpose and feedback.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Kicking skill development: TARGETS: Empty plastic bottles to knock down, pop-up cones, targets with Velcro balls that stick, numbered zones for points, goals with noise makers. GROUND GUIDES: Tape/chalk circle for ball placement, footprint for standing foot, arrow for aim direction, 'kick zone' marked on ball. PROGRESSION: Large target close → smaller target close → large target far → smaller target far → moving targets → game situations. TETHERED KICKING: Ball in net bag on rope — kick, ball returns. Or ball tethered to stake. Allows high repetition without retrieval. FORCE PRACTICE: 'Gentle' zone close, 'hard' zone far — learn to modulate. Different colored targets for force levels.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure targets are stable and won't cause injury when struck. Clear space around kicking area.

item.name: Visual Rule Cards and Game Sequence Strips
item.description: Team games have complex rules that typical children absorb through observation and experience. Children with developmental differences often need rules made explicit and visual. Visual rule cards show what to do in specific situations — 'When you catch the ball: throw to first base.' Game sequence strips show the order of play — 'Batter hits → run to first → if caught, out.' Without this explicit instruction, children may understand individual skills but remain lost during actual games. Visual supports also help in the moment — when confused, child can check the card rather than freezing or doing the wrong thing. This reduces anxiety and increases participation confidence.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating game visual supports: RULE CARDS: For each game, identify key decision points. Create cards: 'IF [situation] THEN [action].' Example: Cricket fielding — 'If you catch the ball in the air, the batter is out.' 'If the ball goes past you, get it and throw to the keeper.' SEQUENCE STRIPS: Show order of game flow. Who does what, in what order. Include what child specifically does at each stage. POSITION GUIDES: Visual diagram of field with child's position marked. Arrows showing where to throw, where to run. WEARABLE: Laminate key rules on wristband or lanyard for in-game reference. BEFORE GAME: Review cards at home. Walk through on empty field. Practice specific situations before they occur in games.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep cards simple and limited to most important rules. Too much information overwhelms.

item.name: Reaction Time Training Games and Equipment
item.description: Sports require fast reactions — the ball is coming, you have a split second to respond. Many children with coordination difficulties have slower reaction times or difficulty processing fast-moving visual information. Reaction time can be improved with practice. Training games and equipment provide repeated opportunities to respond quickly to visual or auditory cues in a fun, low-pressure context. This builds the speed and automaticity that transfers to sports situations. Starting with simple reactions (one response to one cue) and progressing to choice reactions (different responses to different cues) develops the cognitive-motor speed sports demand.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Reaction time training: SIMPLE REACTION (one cue, one response): Clap game — adult claps, child jumps. Drop catch — adult drops ball, child catches before second bounce. Traffic light — 'green' means run, 'red' means stop. CHOICE REACTION (different cues, different responses): Color call — different colors mean different movements. Number slap — call number, child slaps correct card. PROGRESSIVE: Start slow, gradually speed up. Start with one cue, add more. EQUIPMENT: Reaction lights (affordable electronic versions available), slap pads, timer-based games, app-based reaction trainers. TRANSFER: Once basic reaction improves, practice in sports context — 'Ball coming left, move left. Ball coming right, move right.' Build sport-specific reactions.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Match speed to current ability. Start achievable, progress gradually. Avoid frustration from too-hard challenges.

item.name: Balance and Agility Training Equipment
item.description: Sports require dynamic balance — maintaining stability while moving, changing direction, and responding to unpredictable demands. Many sports-struggling children have underlying balance difficulties that compromise all other skills. You can't throw accurately if you're unstable. You can't kick powerfully if you tip over. Agility equipment develops the core stability, proprioceptive awareness, and dynamic balance that underpin sports performance. Balance boards, agility ladders, cones for direction changes, and unstable surfaces all challenge and develop these foundational abilities. Improved balance transfers across all sports activities.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Balance and agility training: BALANCE: Standing on one leg (eyes open → eyes closed). Balance board or wobble cushion activities. Walking on line or beam. Standing while catching/throwing. Dynamic balance during movement. AGILITY: Ladder drills (can use tape on floor instead of buying ladder) — feet in, feet out, hopscotch pattern. Cone weaving. Direction change on command. Start-stop practice. COMBINED: Sports-like movements while maintaining balance — kicking from one foot, throwing while moving, catching while unstable. PROGRESSION: Static balance → dynamic balance → balance during skill → balance in game context. KEY: Many skills that look like coordination problems are actually balance problems. Address balance and coordination often improves.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure safety mats under balance equipment. Supervise closely when children are on unstable surfaces.

item.name: Social Scripts and Team Communication Supports
item.description: Team sports require constant communication — calling for the ball, indicating position, celebrating success, handling mistakes. Children with developmental differences often struggle with this social-motor integration. They may have the physical skills but not the social awareness to use them in team contexts. Social scripts teach the words and phrases used in sports ('I'm open!' 'Pass!' 'Nice shot!' 'My bad!'). Visual supports show when to use them. Practice in low-pressure settings builds confidence for real games. Team communication supports also help children understand teammates' cues and respond appropriately, reducing the social isolation that often accompanies sports struggles.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building sports communication: SCRIPTS TO TEACH: Calling for ball ('Here!' 'Open!' 'Pass!'), encouraging teammates ('Nice!' 'Good try!' 'You got it!'), handling mistakes ('My bad,' 'I'll get it next time,' 'Sorry, team'), asking for help ('What do I do?' 'Where should I go?'). VISUAL SUPPORTS: Cards showing common sports phrases with when to use them. Practice scenarios — 'When you're in position and want the ball, say...'. PRACTICE: Role-play at home. Practice in casual play before competitive games. Video modeling of other children using sports communication. DURING GAME: Wristband with key phrases. Hand signals as alternative to verbal. Buddy who helps prompt communication. KEY: Many children have the physical ability to participate but are excluded because they don't communicate like teammates expect.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Match communication expectations to child's ability. Some children may need alternative communication methods.

socialMedia.caption: The ball bounces off. The swing misses. The kick goes sideways. He's watching from the sideline while other kids play. Sports skills CAN be taught — even when coordination is hard. Here are 9 materials that help with sports and team games: modified balls, visual tracking tools, catching progression equipment, batting tees and hitting aids, kicking targets, visual rule cards, reaction time games, balance and agility equipment, and team communication supports. From sideline to participant. 🏏⚽🎾 #SportsSkills #TeamGames #InclusiveSports #PinnacleBlooms
